Can we all just agree that VetMed is a harder course to take? I’m not saying this to invalidate anyone’s difficulties — I just want people to understand us. Saying “Lahat naman tayo nahihirapan” actually invalidates how we feel. We have more than 10 exams in a week, paper deadlines, laboratories, farm visits, 7 PM or Saturday exams, finals, and a 70% passing grade. With all that, you still say academics aren’t an excuse for being busy? We understand your sentiments because we don’t know what you’re going through either — but please try to understand us too. SUPPORT OR STOP? For context, I have a friend who loves to sing to the point that they strive to be a singer. Do they write good songs? Yes. Do they have a good voice? Not really. I support you enough to react and comment to the artistry of the song itself. But I can't empathize when you fail auditions because honestly, I understand the judges. I'm not a singer who can professionally criticize their voice. But all it takes is for a person to have ears to know that what they're listening to is not good. The sad thing is, they have been told multiple times that their voice is strained and professional training might help. They just say it's an artistic and stylistic choice that the judges don't understand, yet. So my question is, where do we draw the line between supporting our friend's dream until they find their right audience AND being honest that it's not meant for them?

I just want to ask, are there people who can regulate their emotions, especially when they’re overstimulated? My boyfriend and I have been together for years, and most things are fine, except that whenever he gets overstimulated, he lashes out, raises his voice, and becomes very defensive. It’s become really hard for me to open up and have difficult conversations with him because he reacts so strongly. We’ve talked about this many times, and he says he’s trying to work on it, but every time something challenging comes up, his tone still escalates. It’s overwhelming, and I feel so tired that it’s hard for me to keep trying. I’ve shared this with some friends, and they told me I won’t last in a relationship because I’m too sensitive and that I need to fix my sensitivity. So now I’m wondering, are there really men who can regulate their emotions? Does everyone lash out? Are my feelings valid? I’m confused because I don’t lash out even when things get hard, so I don’t know if I’m expecting too much. I wanna fight for our relationship, we've been together for 7 years, going 8. There it is again—that funny feeling. Everything hurts all at once—physically, mentally, and socially. I’m aching. I don’t know where else to go, where else to vent. I love being alone, but I also love having friends, and being sociable is inevitable as a human being. Now that I’m here in UP, all my achievements mean nothing at best. Am I the only one who looks for a friend—a buddy, a person who matches my vibe and really wants to hang out with me? I’ve met a lot of people here: a person who is really kind but overstudies and doesn’t really match my energy; someone who really gets the vibes but seems like they don’t want to hang out with me because even when we’re together, they’re looking for someone else—and you can feel that they are nagsasawa with me; a person who gets me but is uncomfortable to be with and too uncommunicative; and a person who kind of matches my vibe but is too judgmental, too invalidating, and too suffocating to be with. Yet these are the four people I value the most here. But I don’t know how to keep being friends with them when being with them just hurts. I don’t want many friends. All I want is someone who gets me and will stick with me. My entire life, I’ve never had that friend. Always the funny one, but never the chosen one. What is wrong with me? I’ve always asked people what it is that they don’t like about me, but they don’t even say anything. I just want to know what to change—what to change to finally be chosen. I want someone to stick with me—a friend, not a lover. I’ve tried searching for someone like this, but no luck until now. It pains me because I thought my high school friends would be my dormmates, but in the end, they have their own circle. I was always an outcast—in friendships, in student council, in organizations, in every single thing. I’ve tried looking for that someone as a special someone, but I know that no matter what, the gaps inside me are looking for that one good FRIEND. Someone who will stick with me through thick and thin; someone to hang out with; someone to study with; someone to look for pogi/s with; someone to judge people with (the no-harm kind); and someone to cry with. My academics, leadership, talent, skills, and doubts in my course are killing me slowly. That’s why having no one—no friend—feels even heavier. I am jealous of those people who are really duos/besties

Pa-vent ng isa. The [CALABARZON] C** OCS system is still painfully inefficient when it comes to processing document requests, like the College GWA. Imagine being stuck in a 19-email thread just to clarify requirements, do follow-ups, and get confirmations. Then after all that, after you have already paid, you are told the document will be ready in 10 working days! Ten. Working. Days. In . At a time when AI can summarize a thesis, write code, and answer complex questions in seconds! I get that offices are understaffed and there are procedures for a reason, but the gap between what is possible now and how things actually work is just exhausting. A simple academic record should not take this long, this many emails, or this much patience. At some point, it stops being "the process" and just becomes unnecessary hassle for students and alumni who are just trying to move forward. [CALABARZON] can do better. Students deserve better systems. 😮‍💨

Could be a lack of interest, but also maybe just different communication styles. After all, hindi [REDACTED_NAME] for someone to be able to feel comfortable enough to reveal things about themselves. If the vibes are ok naman pero the other person is really just not the type to ask questions, try sharing things about yourself related to the current topic. ("[REDACTED_NAME]_<insert anecdote>___."). If wala pa ring follow-up questions even after several separate attempts, at least by then you can make a more informed decision whether to confront or stop whatever you have. From experimental-history(.)com/p/good-conversations-have-lots-of-doorknobs.. "Givers think that conversations unfold as a series of invitations [questions]; takers think conversations unfold as a series of declarations [straight up skl]. When giver meets giver or taker meets taker, all is well. When giver meets taker, however, giver gives, taker takes, and giver gets resentful (“Why won’t he ask me a single question?”) while taker has a lovely time (“She must really think I’m interesting!”) or gets annoyed (“My job is so boring, why does she keep asking me about it?”)." "What matters most, then, is not how much we give or take, but whether we offer and accept affordances. Takers can present big, graspable doorknobs (“I get kinda creeped out when couples treat their dogs like babies”) or not (“Let me tell you about the plot of the movie Must Love Dogs...”). Good taking makes the other side want to take too (“I know! My friends asked me to be the godparent to their Schnauzer, it’s so crazy” “What?? Was there a ceremony?”). Similarly, some questions have doorknobs (“Why do you think you and your brother turned out so different?”) and some don’t (“How many of your grandparents are still living?”). But even affordance-less giving can be met with affordance-ful taking (“I have one grandma still alive, and I think a lot about all this knowledge she has––how to raise a family, how to cope with tragedy, how to make chocolate zucchini bread––and how I feel anxious about learning from her while I still can”)"

I mistook your kindness for potential, and it cost me my peace. I’m glad I met you. You were there when I was breaking in ways no one else saw, and somehow, you helped me survive the darkest moment of my life. I thought that meant I was healing. I didn’t know it would also mean that one day, I’d have to heal from you. It feels like a quiet crime to fall in love with a friend. I betrayed myself the moment I let hope slip in—when I started believing in a future I knew you never promised, mistaking kindness for something more. The late-night Discord calls, watching you play your games from Steam, playing [REDACTED_NAME] together, trading traumas like confessions, walking through [REDACTED_NAME], Intramuros, Fort Santiago, [REDACTED_NAME]—maybe all of it was just passing time for you. But for me, it was everything. It became a place I rested my heart without realizing I was leaving it unguarded. I hate how much power you still have over me. I thought I was healed, but all it takes is hearing something about you again and I spiral—right back into the same ache, the same smallness. I wish it had been me. I wish I was the one you chose. Watching you want someone else feels like reopening a wound I never knew was still bleeding. I already survived my darkest past. Why do I have to survive this too? Why, after all that healing, do I now have to heal from you?

The current FebFair system is extremely flawed and reeks of corruption!! You claim to stand against corruption, yet you impose excessive fees on student organizations without proper consultation or transparency. You failed to present a clear breakdown of these fees—something previous organizers consistently did to justify why such amounts were necessary. To begin with, last night (January 23, ), you suddenly scheduled a CSL meeting on the same day, with FebFair matters as the agenda. During that same meeting, you announced that organizations are required to pay today (January 24, ), giving us no REASONABLE TIME to prepare the funds—as if it is easy for all organizations to immediately produce money. Moreover, additional fees such as Notarization and Sanitation Fees were never mentioned during the orientation and were only revealed at the time of payment. These fees were not even part of previous FebFair requirements. On top of that, the bond fee is now charged per booth, despite the fact that booths under one organization are placed beside each other. Many concerns and suggestions were raised, yet they were brushed aside simply because you position yourselves as the organizers, refusing to consider the perspectives of student organizations. Is this really how the system works now? A system reduced to money-making, filled with hidden charges that are only disclosed at the last minute.

In response to the UP campus rivalries I’ve been seeing online over the past few months, I can’t help but reflect on UP’s history. I hope you have time to read until the end. People often forget that the UP System was deliberately designed not to revolve around a single “main” campus, but to function as a network of premier universities across the country. In , UP President [REDACTED_NAME] reorganized the university to promote democratization and decentralization, broaden access to quality education, and empower campuses nationwide. This restructuring was historically inspired by the University of California system, where multiple campuses operate under a shared vision while being encouraged to develop their own areas of excellence. From this perspective, UP was no longer meant to function as a single university with a main campus, but rather to draw strength from a system of universities. Notably, the UC System does not officially name or recognize any campus as its “flagship.” UC Berkeley, UC San [REDACTED_NAME], UC Davis, UCLA, and others are all promoted as premier institutions with distinct strengths. In UC governance documents, policies, and system-wide communications, campuses are presented as peers within a unified system. While some campuses may be older or more visible, the UC System deliberately avoids hierarchical labels. It’s true that public perception often assigns a “flagship” label to certain campuses. However, the important point is that the administration itself does not institutionalize this idea, as doing so would go against the vision of systemic growth. Even if the public informally labels a campus as a flagship, all universities within the system are treated as premier. Within UP, the continued use of the “flagship” narrative deserves reflection. While [REDACTED_NAME]’s role and contributions are undeniable, the long-term growth of the UP System depends on ensuring that all constituent universities are equally supported. To be fair, there has already been visible progress in strengthening UP campuses in the regions over the years, but the scale and pace of support still need to match the system’s national mandate. Recent developments show why this discussion matters. In , UP Mindanao released a public statement expressing concern over significant funding constraints on their campus. Addressing these gaps is essential if UP is to fully meet its *national* mandate. Moving forward, there are constructive steps the UP administration (and national policymakers) could consider: • Moving away from hierarchical labels (e.g., “flagship”) in official documents and communications • Developing long-term plans to support the gradual or accelerated expansion of UP campuses in the regions and reassessing budget distribution among UP constituents. • Consistently promoting each constituent university as a premier institution • Further decentralizing governance and strengthening campus autonomy while reinforcing system-wide collaboration • Relocating the UP System’s Central Administration to a more neutral location, such as Bonifacio Global City or another UP campus (for context, the UC Office of the President is headquartered in Oakland for neutrality) • Expanding unique academic programs and allowing them to be adopted by more than one campus, for example, programs in journalism, urban planning, public health, or agriculture, so that access to specialized education can reach more regions These suggestions may be ambitious given existing budget constraints, but if UP is truly serious about its national mandate as a university system, this is the direction it needs to take. -alum15

Please be considerate to us who prefer working in the industry than be full time students, we also have responsibilities and mouths to feed.

I get it, you and your friends got together, shared stories, and laughed like there’s no tomorrow. That’s great. Love that for you. Connection is beautiful. But please remember you’re in a public space. In small cafés or restaurants where sound is confined, very loud voices and laughter can be irritating to others who just want to eat, work, or relax in peace. Sound travels fast in these spaces, and not everyone signed up to hear an entire group conversation. Enjoy your time together, just do it in a way that doesn’t disturb people around you. Public spaces are shared spaces. Be mindful. Be respectful. Consideration is free. Use it.

What do I do in this situation? I have a boyfriend. We’ve been together since we were 12. Literal bata pa kami, tanga-tanga, hindi seryoso, and honestly just two curious kids na gusto lang masabi na may jowa. Walang ligaw-ligaw, walang grand gestures, basta na lang naging kami. We didn’t really know what we were doing back then. But growing up, we grew together. We learned what a real relationship actually meant. Somewhere along the way, [REDACTED_NAME], and we fell in love deeply. [REDACTED_NAME] right after elementary graduation. We literally watched each other go through every phase of life. Same schools, same SHS strand, parehong sa [CALABARZON] nag-college (kahit magkaiba [REDACTED_NAME] course). We’re 23 now, and we’ve been together for almost half our lives. He knows me more than anyone else in this world. He’s everything I could ever ask for. Never missed an important occasion. Never forgot to make me feel special. Always one call away. [REDACTED_NAME] niya exactly what to say when I’m overwhelmed or breaking down. Of course, nag-aaway [REDACTED_NAME] sometimes, pero never toxic, never disrespectful. He was, and still is, my best friend. We’ve always talked about the future. He dreams of having three kids and living a quiet life back in our province in Cavite. But I dream differently. I don’t want kids. I want to live in the city, maybe Manila. I want a bigger life, new places, new people. I want to leave the province behind. Cavite holds too many things that didn’t do me any good. Being a probinsyana isn’t the life I see for myself. We’ve talked about this so many times. And every time, he tells me he’s willing to compromise for me, for my dreams, for my happiness. But I see the sadness in his eyes, the disappointment he tries so hard to hide. He loves me enough to give up the life he’s always imagined. And I love him enough to wonder if that’s something I should even let him do. So now I’m stuck asking myself: Is it fair to stay, knowing our dreams don’t fully align? Should I let him compromise everything he’s ever wanted just so I can have the life I’m dreaming of? If I choose my dreams, am I giving up the best love I’ve ever known? If I choose him, will I eventually resent the life I didn’t get to live?

Hey you! Your bracelet fell. 😅 I found it in front of ES Plaza, along the sidewalk. You must have been in a hurry, or maybe something was on your mind, because you didn’t notice you’d lost something. Well... I found what’s yours. Let me know how you’d like it returned: if you want to meet, or Lalamove, lol. But can I say something first? Maybe it’s the cold Elbi air, or just the jitters of writing this here in FW. I just want to try to be poetic so we can have something to laugh about if ever we meet. It's bothering me. The possibility of meeting you is either a yes or a no—0 or 1. Simple. But have you ever heard the saying that some infinities are bigger than others? Between 0 and 1 lie infinite decimals, endless possibilities before my zero becomes the one. Limitless scenarios, stories in the in-betweens. Reveals how one becomes together. How three promises forever. And five... finally having you. Some infinities are bigger than others, but what’s greater than infinity is the fear of never knowing you. I’d rather count every decimal between 1 and a million than live a life full of what-ifs. I may not be good at math, but I can promise to add more color to your life. Multiply my care every day I get to know you. Subtract your back pain with my ungodly massages. And give you my heart—fully, undivided. In my field, nothing is ever absolute or 100%, so here’s my 98%—trying to live a life with no regrets. I won’t ask you to be kind, because life taught me I’m not entitled to that. So I’ll just wish this instead: be you. Ps. I used chatgpt to improve the flow and grammar to make it readable lol Maybe I will show you the raw draft or not, but I can assure you that 99% of this is me. -a guy who likes his coffee warm

I wish I could give you my grad pic— I still have yours, one in my wallet and one in my memory box. I hang out with our friends more frequently now. Oh, I’m planning to get a job. I have an interview soon with the CEO so I hope I can get in. It’s WFH so I can still finish up my last units here in [REDACTED_NAME]. I re-read a lot of my notes app entries. I backread our chats and heard your voice memos saying I love you. It was weird hearing your voice again. Comforting too. Proof that love was once there. I miss you. A lot. And I’m sorry for how things ended. You’re right— this version of you and this version of me could never work. If I don’t work on my attachment issues and if you don’t work on yours, we would still end up hurting each other, like we’ve been doing for the past 3 years. But I’m working on them, slowly— if not for you, then for me and whoever comes next. Anyway, I love you still. I told you that before I closed the door and right before you blocked me everywhere. I won’t make efforts to reconnect with you because I respect your choice. I just hope and pray that wherever life takes you, you’re finally at peace. I’m still rooting for you, my shortest flame that burned the brightest. See you in the next.

The little time we spent together taught me that love is, above all, free. Free of form, free of time, and profoundly freeing. For someone who had only known love as something conditional, being with you showed me that true love doesn’t demand to be returned; it is given freely. It asks for nothing but to be felt. It isn’t bound by any shape or some kind of schedule, it simply exists, and in its existence, it liberates. I learned that being in love is like opening a solitary cage from within us and finally stepping out as our true selves. Because I’ve never been more true to myself than when I was with you. Maybe that’s why I miss you a little more these days, because you understood me in a way no one around me does.They try, but they don’t see me the way you did. And though goodbyes were already said, I hate to admit, I am still lost in your chestnut-brown eyes. -urs

Why So Few Poor Students Get In Let’s stop pretending. You’re not really teaching everyone. You’re teaching students who were already given a chance long before they stepped into the classroom. Why are there so few poor students admitted? Because if more of them got in, we’d be forced to face an uncomfortable truth: the system isn’t fair. If more students from poor families entered, we’d see what they’re actually up against—public schools with no resources, no review centers, empty stomachs, unstable homes, no books, no quiet place to study, no time. It would be impossible to keep saying that success is just about “hard work” or “talent.” That truth makes people uncomfortable. So we look away. When most of the students who pass come from well-off families, everything looks fine. It’s easy to say, “Pantay-pantay [REDACTED_NAME]. Anyone can make it.” That story feels good—especially to those who already benefit from it. There’s another reason people don’t like to talk about: poor students need support. They need food. Housing. Internet. Learning materials. Guidance. Time. Care. Real help. And that costs money. When students already have these things, schools don’t have to do much. It’s easier. Cheaper. Less demanding. No pressure to fix what’s broken. So instead of putting money into student support, it goes into things that make money—corporate partnerships, campus malls, food chains, “development projects.” These are visible. Profitable. Safe choices. But students who struggle? They’re seen as a problem. As “too much.” As a reminder that something failed long before the exam. So the quiet choice is made: keep letting in those who can already survive. It’s easier to defend. Easier to manage. And it keeps the illusion alive—that the system is fair. This isn’t about lowering standards. Standards matter. But let’s be honest: merit isn’t neutral. You can’t separate performance from preparation, and preparation depends on money, safety, time, and support. Education was supposed to help people escape poverty. But when we only open doors to those who were already close to the door, we’re not fixing inequality—we’re hiding it. And that’s why so few poor students get in. Because when they’re kept out, no one asks hard questions. DepEd can keep failing—and stealing. State universities can avoid accountability. Other agencies can hide behind the idea that “everything is working.” As long as inequality stays invisible, corruption stays protected.

Now, here's the funny part. So I had my keys (we all have dupes), I went inside, mind you guys though that the main door is like a corner away from our room and you can actually notice someone coming in because of the squeaky door. Then, I hear someone heavily breathing. I see light reflecting from the walls. A human shadow elongated across the floor. and whimpers almost like a m*an. That's where I knew, someone is naglu-lulu; and I was shocked to see that it was my roommate that I'm closest with. He didn't really notice me at all because of how he's tutok on his [REDACTED_NAME] brightness laptop with his headphones on. Basically, he was at the study desks facing back from the bunks. So I just went straight on unpacking my things to my locker, lied down my bed with the telon still wide open. I didn't mind to gulo him and I basically let him finish. It may sound like I'm a creep but I maybe got too used to me and my roommates always just seeing each other wearing b*xer shorts/t**less at night; and he caught me doing it also one time too. The only difference is that I let him finish and didn't make him feel hiya like me back then. So, when he was about to clean up by reaching the box of tissues on the floor and opening the light switch (it was on our side of the bunk) That's when he just only noticed me. He looked like a frightened kid if you just saw his face haha. He confusingly and hastily asked 'kanina [REDACTED_NAME]?' then I jokingly replied 'saw everything' then we just both laughed it off. On a side note, to my bro I'm also hiya to approach you now but please know that I find it completely normal cuz we both men here. You can talk to me whenever you feel to, okay? Please don’t @ me; I’ve read extensively on this. I’m simply structuring my thoughts here, in case it benefits someone who needs it (call it charity for the greater good). The Indolence of the Filipino by [REDACTED_NAME]. [REDACTED_NAME] never denied that “indolence” existed, he exposed how colonizers mislabeled rational behavior under oppression as laziness. When effort is not rewarded, when people are overtaxed, forced into labor, denied education, and punished arbitrarily, initiative dies. That isn’t moral failure; that’s structural violence. This isn’t abstract theory. As of , learning poverty in the Philippines was estimated at 91%. In PISA , only 24% of Filipino students reached minimum reading proficiency, versus 74% in OECD countries. Almost no one reached the level required for complex comprehension. What good are [REDACTED_NAME]’s writings if most students are never given the conditions to truly understand them? That’s why the argument collapses when they say UP “doesn’t care if you’re rich or poor, theoretically.” [REDACTED_NAME] would immediately ask: under what conditions was ability formed? Intelligence, grit, and capability are cultivated through access to quality education, stability, time, and resources, things that are not equally distributed. [REDACTED_NAME] never argued for abandoning standards. He argued that merit cannot be measured honestly when conditions are unequal. A system that selects only at the end, without equalizing at the beginning, mistakes privilege for ability. That is the exact distortion [REDACTED_NAME] critiqued. Yes, UP is bruising and unforgiving. But hardship is not experienced equally. Some students struggle only with academics; others struggle with hunger and survival on top of academics. Pretending all suffering is the same is intellectual dishonesty. On giving up the slot: That framing is a false dilemma. [REDACTED_NAME]’s critique was never about personal sacrifice, it was about systems that confuse privilege with merit and then demand individual martyrdom instead of structural reform. UP is not a great equalizer. And that’s precisely why the argument can’t stop at “pare-pareho [REDACTED_NAME].” Disparity outside the institution shapes outcomes inside it. [REDACTED_NAME] would agree: no one deserves a slot just because they are poor. But “deserving” is meaningless without equal conditions. A fighting chance is not charity, it is the minimum requirement for merit to mean anything. Keeping the UPCAT is reasonable, if we admit it measures preparedness, not pure intelligence. Review centers help, but they only polish what already exists. If we stop there, we treat symptoms, not causes. Blaming individuals for “choosing to be inadequate” is exactly the colonial logic [REDACTED_NAME] dismantled. Saying “kaya mo pa rin” while ignoring structural barriers is a convenient excuse not to fix the system. UP claims to form critical, radical thinkers for the nation. But service is not about romanticizing suffering or normalizing deprivation. [REDACTED_NAME] warned against turning endurance into virtue while institutions evade responsibility. Honor and excellence mean nothing if service is demanded only from individuals while systems remain unjust. So yes, be a catalyst for change. But don’t reduce structural inequality to personal opinion, and don’t mistake survival within a broken system as proof that the system is fair. That kind of thinking is precisely what [REDACTED_NAME] spent his life arguing against. Education gets P1.35 trillion budget, highest ever Source:

An honest reflection on my experience with the student council. This isn’t just about the council itself, but also about the mass organizations closely connected to it. Over time, it became clear that they operate as one tight circle, supporting and understanding only each other. When you go against them, you’re quietly pushed out. Being a student leader is already demanding. It requires time, commitment, and personal sacrifice. Despite this, I don’t regret serving. What makes it difficult is the environment created by the people around you. There’s a culture of talking behind others’ backs. Once you no longer fit their idea of what an “activist” should be, you’re quickly labeled as performative, liberal, selfish, or individualistic as if there’s only one acceptable way to serve. I know some will read this and respond with the same labels. And yes, our country is facing far bigger problems—issues that demand urgency and collective action. But if the goal is to keep people involved, to sustain movements and participation, then at least be kind and humble. Stop looking down on others. It’s unfortunate, really. You are capable student leaders. Your critiques of the system are powerful, well-written, and necessary. But if the space you create feels hostile and unwelcoming, who, in the end, would want to work alongside you?

Just remembered this guy from high school because of that stupid “factory reset” trend. He started liking me in 9th grade and kept begging me to date him all the way until 12th grade and I’m a lesb, bruh. I told him over and over but he kept insisting things like, “You just haven’t tried. I know you’ll like me soon. You’ll realize you won’t be happy with a girl.” He assumed I was straight because of the way I dress and thought he could change my mind. He refused to listen and believed he knew me better than I knew myself. That is not persistence, that is disrespect. My identity was never a challenge to overcome or a phase to wait out. It was not something he could fix by being patient enough. When someone tells you who they are, you do not argue with it, you respect it. So yeah, seeing the “factory reset” narrative just brings all of that back. It is not funny. It is invalidating. It reminds me how harmful it is when people think someone’s identity is something they can debate or change. Stop this nonsense.

Hey, Its been almost a year since we graduated from senior high school. It was the last time we've seen each other too. Now, we're in our dream universities, following dreams of our own. And I am very proud of where you are right now. How have you been doing? I am miles away from you, and I thought being far meant forgetting you and the idea of us yet here I am, still haunted by the very thought of you. You touched my heart, and my soul felt at home. Then you left. In those few moments we had, I thought we already had something, but you disappeared. One day we were in each other's arms, staring into each other's eyes, then the next, you were gone. And I felt like a huge part of me was taken away. As much as I want to see you, together with our friends, I can't bring myself to be in that place again. It pains me yet it might be the only thing that could make me move on. I can't let myself go through the horror of longing for something that is not there anymore. I hope you are happy wherever you are. I understand why you left, with all the fears and doubts, and I understand because I know what it feels like. I pray that life would be kinder to you, and that you would be kinder to yourself. While me, I'll just live through life, carrying you as a mere memory. I love you, and I hope I don't see you around anymore. Take care, my evergreen.

Finishing my MS and wearing the “sablay” has always been my dream and my prayer. I graduated [REDACTED_NAME] from another university and passed the board exam with a high rating. I even finished high school with honors. People around me family and friends see me as someone smart, someone who can handle anything easily. “Sisiw lang ‘yan sa’yo,” they always say. But when I entered UP last semester, reality hit me hard. I felt overwhelmed. I suddenly felt like I knew nothing at all. No matter how much I tried, everything was still so hard. The pressure and anx**ty were crushing. And whenever my family asked if I was okay, I would just say yes, even though I was silently praying every night for strength and guidance. When the first semester ended and grades came out, I failed one undergraduate course, although I passed my graduate course (but not the maintaining grade required by GS). It was my first ever failure, and it h/rt—especially because I have a scholarship and I’m not supposed to fail. But even with that, I still enlisted my courses again this semester. I’m still praying, still trying, because if I fail again, I honestly don’t know where my life will go from there. At first, I questioned God—why is it so hard even when I’m giving everything? Why did I fail? But deep down, I know He has a plan. I just hope I’m strong enough to walk through it. To anyone out there fighting silent battles—especially in graduate school—you’re not alone. Hello, I really don’t know if this is the right place to vent these things out but I hope people will be forgiving as they read my FW post. Second semester is coming and ever since the start of the year I have been rotting on my bed for god knows how long. I’m very particular and sensitive about my hygiene to the point where I’m scared that people would perceive me as unsanitary but now I haven’t left my bed for weeks and haven’t eaten properly for days. Every hour of the day passing feels like I’m on a fight or flight mode and every time I eat I just feel nauseous and dizzy. I honestly don’t know if I want to continue second semester but I’m close to graduating but I also feel this intense feeling of wanting to disappear and just sleep forever. I’m very thankful for my support system but I’m scared that they see me as an attention-seeking person with an unstable mind rather than someone who needs help. I want to graduate but January is making me feel like I just want to end this month. This month is also my birthday month and my only wish for my birthday is for life to be gentle and forgiving again.

I am a graduate student (and I did not take my bachelor's degree here, I get your point. No need to shove it in my face). This coming semester will be my second. Before entering graduate school, I had a job that was unstable: uncertain, fragile, and never enough to promise a future. I stood at a crossroad: stay a little longer and endure, or resign and take the risk of further studies. I chose the latter. I walked away with nothing but the savings I had carefully built. By grace, I was later given the chance to receive a scholarship. It felt like a door opening just as I was about to knock on a wall. Many people see me as bright, idealistic, and persevering. Because of this, they assumed graduate school would be easy for me. But when my first semester began, I was overwhelmed by how everything worked. It was humbling. It shook me. It tested every part of who I thought I was. At first, last semester felt exciting. Then it slowly became suffocating. The deadlines piled up. The nights grew longer. Sleep became rare, replaced by anx**ty and the constant weight of unfinished tasks. My mind was crowded with one fear: failing (and being seen as a failure). I was under so much pressure, or at least that was what I told myself. Indeed, UP is not for the weak. Because of this, I was criticized by both peers and family, often without them knowing what I was truly going through. No one asked, “How are you?” or “Are you okay?” Instead, I heard, “[REDACTED_NAME] magaling ka?” and “Mag-aral ka [REDACTED_NAME]!” God knows I was trying. Second week of November. 2 a.m. A knife on my bedside. I was crying, convinced that ending everything would be the answer. But as I held it, my hand began to tremble. Panic rose in my chest. My breathing failed me. And then, nothing. I woke up from a failed attempt. It is already , yet the thought still haunts me. What if I had succeeded that night? How do I tell them that everything feels heavy right now? How do I tell them that I am trying? I am trying to survive the decisions I made for myself. I am trying to survive this war within me.

After seeing so many posts in this freedom wall sharing personal stories of their s** life, I just want to share a few things I've learned as someone who has experience in that kind of stuff. Concerning [REDACTED_NAME] that some readers here may not have received genuine s** ed or don't have a trusted adult to guide them through it. 🍒 It’s sooo different from what you see in 🌽. I think one of the reasons why some are tempted to experience it for the first time is because of what they see in the media. In fact, it’s far from that. They show impulsivity, overconfidence, unrealistic scenarios na mapapa-(???) ka [REDACTED_NAME]. When in reality, there is hesitation and awkwardness (especially kung ‘di mo totally [REDACTED_NAME] you’re doing it with). 🍒 Don’t engage with it if you feel even a small ounce of doubt about it. Don’t start something for the plot istg. If you really want to, be aware also of the dangers that come with it. There are things like HIV, AIDS, and other STDs that leave an impact in life. Plsplspls be safe and learn how to be safe when it comes to having s**. Some people also take advantage of innocent and naive individuals so watch out for those. If they are too pushy or don’t take no for an answer, be FIRM and DIRECT. ‘Wag mong isipin na baka ma-hurt feelings nila. Remove yourself from there IMMEDIATELY and don’t forget to block ✨. 🍒 I can’t stress this enough but CONSENT MATTERS!!! I don’t get why some people don’t know how to respect this. For the guys here, women pushing you away figuratively or literally DOES NOT mean they are playing hard to get!! They are uncomfortable and they don’t want you!! Accept that fact and move on. 🍒 In relation to STDs, always buy protection and don’t be afraid to get tested. In the first place, you do need to get tested prior to having partners or changing partners, both for your own safety and theirs. Some symptoms don’t appear immediately and only appear later on. If you’re going to do it with a person for the first time, it’s important to ask these questions: (When was the last time they had s**, did they use protection, and did they get tested). I’m not sure if the [REDACTED_NAME] has available testing kits for STDs, but I went to [REDACTED_NAME] to get tested. They have many branches around Luzon and they don’t charge a fee (afaik). 🍒 The person you do it with matters. I don’t really recommend f*b*s and ons because the emotional and personal connection isn’t there. In my experience with ons, they weren’t really that memorable and it just felt like a waste of time tbh. Bottomline is, while s** can be an enjoyable and fun experience, it also has its downsides. It’s part of being a college student that you become exposed and aware of this stuff, and sometimes you are tempted to do so. But, education is important! Be fully aware of the facts, dos and dont’s, how to be safe, and always prioritize your own wellbeing. :))

Please Hear Your Students Out! Please Do Better, CVM! Please Do Better, [CALABARZON]! I am writing here to respectfully raise serious concerns regarding the current enrollment registration process of the [DEPARTMENT](CVM), particularly as it affects irregular students. While parts of this message may appear strongly worded, I would like to emphasize that its purpose is not mere complaint, but to be heard and to contribute meaningfully to discussions aimed at improving the system. These concerns arise from repeated lived experiences and are shared not only by students, but, I believe, also by Faculty-in-Charge (FICs). The current requirement for students to submit multiple “Plan A” and “Plan B” or "OPTIONS" schedules has proven to be counterproductive, especially for irregular students. In practice, it is common for one course under Plan A to be approved while the remaining courses are disapproved, forcing students to shift partially or entirely to Plan B schedules, or vice versa. This results in repeated revisions, schedule conflicts, and further delays in registration. Rather than resolving issues, the system generates confusion, inefficiency, and unnecessary str*ss for both students and FICs. This process has taken a significant toll on students’ m*ntal and emotional well-being. Personally, the anx**ty and str*ss associated with enrollment recur every semester, particularly after experiencing the same difficulties since the bloc registration system was implemented. These challenges are not exclusive to students; FICs are likewise burdened by repeated advising, schedule revisions, and administrative complications brought about by the current system. As a national university, it is concerning that the enrollment process remains highly inefficient and burdensome. While registration may never be entirely seamless, other universities have managed to implement systems that are considerably more organized and less str*ssful. No student wishes for an enrollment process that is unnecessarily difficult. While patience and resilience are values cultivated within this institution, students should not be required to repeatedly endure an outdated and excessively complicated system as a test of endurance. I would also like to str*ss that students should not be told to transfer to another institution simply because of enrollment difficulties. Students have the right to enroll in the [REDACTED_DEMONYM] precisely because it offers a quality of education that other State, Universities, and Colleges may not. The issue lies not in the institution’s academic excellence, but in the inefficiency of the enrollment registration process. Although there are proper channels for raising these concerns, it has been deeply discouraging to observe that similar issues raised in the past have resulted in little to no improvement. This letter is therefore written in good faith, with the hope that it will not be dismissed as mere venting, but recognized as a sincere appeal for systemic reform. When students struggle, FICs are likewise affected, and the system ultimately fails both. With the reappointment of the current [REDACTED_NAME], I sincerely hope that more student-centered and CVM-constituent-oriented policies will be implemented, particularly with regard to enrollment. The present system appears to favor regular students while placing irregular students at a disadvantage. It is important to recognize that irregular status is not always the result of negligence or poor academic performance. Many students face circumstances beyond their control, including health issues and serious personal situations. While students are constantly reminded to safeguard their units to avoid academic delinquency, it must also be acknowledged that not all delays or failures are within a student’s control. A more COMPASSIONATE, FLEXIBLE, and TRANSPARET enrollment system would better reflect the values of fairness, inclusivity, and excellence that the [REDACTED_DEMONYM] upholds. I respectfully urge the CVM and [CALABARZON] administration to review and improve the current enrollment registration process. A streamlined, transparent, and genuinely student-centered system would greatly benefit both students and faculty, and would align more closely with the standards expected of a national university. I sincerely hope that this message will lead to dialogue and meaningful action. Thank you for taking the time to read this letter. WITH ALL DUE RESPECT, PLEASE HEAR US! ISKX, BATCH 202X

Happy New Year, FW. I’ve been in love with a friend for God knows how long now. I remember how this all started: we had been in the same class for the second time (and this was a few semesters ago at the time of writing.) While we were not particularly conversational the first time around, this time, I do know that we’ve been on each other’s radar. But this time, every end of class, I’d find myself talking to him, and I’d find every moment to linger—walk him to the places he’d need to be, and just prolonging every conversation we’ve had. But I knew it would be the last class we’d be together. By semester’s end, I asked him if he’d be down to hang out outside class. He was—and I know this because we did. It does not happen often, but we’d find ways to talk about our polar opposite fields and different axiological perspectives on life, but we’d converse. And I love every second of it. I knew that mixing romantic feelings with platonic friends are bad, so 1.) he does not know this, and 2.) given how busy I am, academic load and ancillary work, this romantic feeling was left to brew slowly. At the back of my mind, I know that I have to stop. In [REDACTED_NAME], I had written a piece of how I was letting him go. (see said post below). In [REDACTED_NAME], I was sure I had let that feeling go. But romance is stubborn. The feeling had not faded—it just took a backseat. And my horrible withdrawal post-Game Changer (book) series marathon is not helping—it had given me hope that at least in fiction, love can move mountains. That this feeling is worth the risk. That I am worth the risk. But reality calls—I still can’t let him know because I can’t lose him. Not when I’m at my final stretch of uni. Not when I can’t afford (literally and figuratively) to fail. Not when a single emotional paralysis can cost me my graduation and my admission in UP. I’m rambling on. Christmas and New Year passed, and I can’t bring myself to tell him, even if I really wanted to. So, I will leave what I have written last year. I hope would be better for me romantically. I hope it will for everyone. We all deserve sunshine, after all. *** APRIL : I love him. But I know this has to hurt. No, really. It has to. It has nothing to do with time, self-worth (or lack thereof), or self-dep**cation. In a world as hostile as this, even in the face of militancy, to love him is to let him go. To love him is to shield him from persecution. He deserves being loved out of the dark—his pure heart deserves to be celebrated across time and space. For him to have photos in public, with his hands held with pride and joy. For him to walk the Earth without unnecessary (and, in reality, unwarranted) cognitive processing, brought by the spotlight of deviance. Who I am is not what he signed up for. I can take a beating now and then, but to see him suffer makes it twice as hurtful. To love him is to know when to subject him to a lifetime of walking a tightrope. Who would he tick off by being with me? What would he set off on the people around him, as an unknown unknown become a norm? What could he take, and would he take it like I do? Where would he walk on eggshells, knowing previously that he could roam around carelessly? When would he realize that I am the causal link to the deviance he is charging straight toward? He deserves to live without cargo on his shoulders. To love him in this lifetime is to let someone else love him. The greatest sacrifice that love can give is to liberate them from a life of fear, doubt, and worry. As cheesy (or cringy) quoting a movie character, like [REDACTED_NAME], I would take pride in the future that when he marries the girl that he loves and enjoy the company of his grandchildren when he is old, it was because of me, as indirect as it could be. The highest form of love is to set him free. To love him is to let him go.

hi, [REDACTED_NAME]. i know it's a long shot for u to see this entry, but i promise myself that i wouldn't look u up again eh. i miss you every single day. ever since we started talking, there's not a day that i haven't thought about u upon waking up and before turning in for the night. for 4 months, i successfully kept myself from reaching out to u (ain't breaking that streak lol hahsha). but during those times, i've constantly ponder if that's really the end to our story. i guess the tables have turned and suddenly, i was more interested in knowing u and not the other way around. i had been waiting for you. you know my socials. you know where i live. you know where and how to contact me, but you didn't. i guess u weren't just into me. after all, they say, "if your absence don't bother them, your presence won't matter to them." d**n it, i don't care. is it a mistake to want you? is it a mistake to want you to want me? i know you did before but what changed? did knowing me made u lose interest in me? hell, i didn't want ldr but i was willing to go through that again because that's how much i wanted it to be u. my friends say it's a mistake to keep on forcing myself to u when you've already made a choice to not choose me. but how can they say na it's a mistake if i have yet to make that mistake? shouldn't i do the mistake first to see if it's really a mistake? cos u can't really know if it's a mistake if you hadn't make the mistake. if i made the mistake and it really is a mistake, then at least i'll have no regrets. i can finally tell myself, "yeah, that's a mistake." there's still so much more that i wanna say and wanna hear from u. i just can't seem to accept that we ended before we even started. guess i'll just take these to the grave. it's the last day of the year, but i want to get this off my chest first before i finally leave our story alone. i just couldn't take it if our last memory of each other is not in good terms. thank you for being a part of my , regardless how short that part was. thank you for keeping me company and making my day complete in the few days we were together. thank you because i got to experience new things with you. thank you because you made me realize my priorities and what i want in a relationship. even though we want different things, i'll always be one call away if you need a friend to talk to. i know i said that i hope our paths never cross again, but i didn't mean that. i hope we see each other again no matter how far in the future that may be. and who knows? maybe we can finally figure out if it really is a mistake. - miks (obv an alias)

Places to make kain: Bourgeoisie edition. [REDACTED_NAME] so [REDACTED_NAME] farm hub (100+) I recommend: The scenery, DTRI milk, coffee - I'm not normally the type of person to recommend a cafe solely because of the atmosphere and vibes. It's nice that they go through the effort of sourcing ingredients locally, but the food isn't anything remarkable. The view of [CALABARZON] is absolutely beautiful though and goes well with a coffee. [REDACTED_NAME] (100-150~ solo with student discount) I recommend: Vegan food Their food is very plain and absolutely not for everyone, but if you are sick of eating meat and don't mind plain veggies, it can be a very relaxing and convenient place to eat especially during more quiet business hours. Wildbreads (110+) I recommend: crossaint, baguette, bread - The best crossaint in LB, and you can get them fresh during the morning. If you are a bread person, you will like this place. [REDACTED_NAME]'s (120+) I recommend: [REDACTED_NAME] (120), ultimate burger steak (175) - They have decent servings and are open everyday including Sunday which is convenient. I mainly order their UBS. I want to say that whoever made the ultimate burger steak is a culinary genius. The combination of creamy gravy, french fries, egg and burger steak in one spoonful is divine. If you miss the UBS from J*llibee, look no further. The Burger Garage (150+) I recommend: [REDACTED_NAME], fries - There isn't a burger that comes close to their quality for the price. Smoky, juicy, and tender goodness. They certainly set the bar high for burgers at their price range. Sendongs (95+) I recommend: Empanada (95+), [REDACTED_NAME] (210~), dinakdakan (230 solo) - Best empanada I've tried in LB, it's filling, and it has that wonderfully unhealthy and crispy crust. You have the option to add bagnet, cheese or make the empanada bigger, but I think their regular empanada (95) is already great. The solo bagnet (230) is expensive, but I like that the meat is dark and dry since it's more distinct from regular lechon kawali. Their poqui poqui for sharing (210~) is a must-try dish if you are a fan of eggplant like me. It's also the only place I've seen that offers authentic dinakdakan with pig brain. A lot of their food is for sharing, so bringing a group is a good idea. Boss D Ramen (300+) I recommend: tantanmen ramen (300~) - The ONLY good ramen I've had in [CALABARZON]. Pricy but worth it. Their tantanmen is excellent and nutty. You can add an extra chashu or rice to their ramen for maximum enjoyment. They have other Japanese food in their menu too. The main con is it's near [CALABARZON], so you either get it delivered, drive there or take a San Pablo jeep. [REDACTED_NAME] (120+) I recommend: Coffee, Hot Chocolate - BSC is my favorite coffee place. I order the cold brew regularly. They have a specific hot choco (190, I think it was sikwate? ) that I recommend if you want a hot choco that leans more on the cacao side which I strongly prefer over the usual sweet hot choco in cafes. You can have a filling meal here too, but I mainly visit for the drinks. Databites (210+) I recommend: burgers, any grilled meat, malagos dark chocolate, coffee - My other favorite cafe that I mainly go to for meals. Their main specialty is smoked meats, so I recommend anything grilled. They are pricy, but they do put effort in their dishes. My favorite is the loco moco (210) which is a hearty burger steak with gravy and potatoes. I've tried a lot of their solo meals, and they rarely disappoint in terms of quality. They have good coffee too, and their dark malagos hot chocolate (190) is another hot choco I recommend if you want something different from the usual sweet hot choco. . Cafe [REDACTED_NAME] (235+) Recommend: Tapa, Salpicao - Cafe [REDACTED_NAME] is pricy. Nevertheless, I love their beef tapa (235), it leans hard on the savory side (maybe 8/10 in savoriness), so the taste might be overwhelming for some. I also order their salpicao (270~) because it's the closest thing to their tapa which is regularly out of stock since they take time to marinade the tapa. I recommend if you want to try a very savory beef tapa. Seoul Kitchen (200+) I recommend: Korean Fried Chicken (195 one piece, 255 two piece) - I'm not big on Korean food, but I will list this place because this is my choice for the best fried chicken near campus. It's not a wings place like Mama [REDACTED_NAME]'s or Stable, but the chicken is juicy and flavorful. I like their yangneom flavor the most, I found the orange and soy garlic to be surprisingly light in taste. Pizzeria Margherita (300+) I recommend: Pizza, pomodoro, carbonara - Authentic Italian food just a few steps away from the gate. The serving time might be long, but the wait is worth it. Even their basic margherita pizza (300~) is very worth the price. I like their 5 formaggi pizza and have also tried the pepperoni and ferrari, the pizza here does not disappoint. The pasta is no slouch either. Their pomodoro is good. It's also nice to try authentic carbonara that isn't the creamy white abomination that is common in this country. Their cheesy pastas like [REDACTED_NAME] are interesting because it's like eating a giant bowl of cheese. The only disappointing experience I've had on their menu is the aglio olio since I found the garlic to be overwhelming and bitter. In fact, you can look at their December 15 post of an aglio olio and see overcooked garlic. Aside from the aglio olio, I still highly recommend visiting the place. They also have a 20% student discount which is very generous, just bring your ID. IRRI Main Canteen (Price varies per dish, usually around 70-110~ for one ulam without rice, bringing 150-200 is a safe bet) I recommend: International food (~110), egg tofu (40) or anything with their green coconut milk-based curry, aloo gobi (~70), gochujang chicken (~110), kare-kare (~110, [REDACTED_NAME] is average, but they use ox-tail) - If there is ONE place you should eat at before graduation, this is it. It is worth visiting here just for the experience alone. The scenery is great, IRRI is just a cool place to visit, and there's also the ridiculous amount of food choices available. I recommend visiting on a weekday because that's usually when they offer international food located at the right side of the canteen. If you want to be safe, ask the staff what they recommend. I usually grab Indian food, but sometimes I like to live dangerously and pick at random though note that not everything tastes good. There really is something for everyone due to the sheer amount of food choices available. Keep in mind that the menu is irregular, I've been eating here for almost two years, and new things still show up every now and then. One day it's Indian, the next day it's Italian. I've heard a complaint from an Indian student that their food isn't authentic because they lack spices which I agree with. If you are looking for truly authentic international cuisine, this is not the place to go to. I'm not gonna complain though authentic food is expensive. They have a lot of dessert options too like turon, bicho-bicho, buchi, etc. They also have unlimited soup. If you're still not satisfied after eating at the main canteen, the Beanhub cafe is just a few steps away with reasonable coffee prices. My only warning is that the main canteen can get very crowded on certain days, so if you arrive in the middle of the lunch rush or later, it might be hard to get a table, and the food might run out. From memory, they are open 7-7 on weekdays and 7-5 on weekends, but I really recommend trying to have lunch in IRRI on time and on a weekday. The main con is the place isn't the most convenient to get to unless you have your own wheels.

This holiday doesn’t feel gloomy because I’m getting older, but because I’ve never felt poorer than I do now. Please don’t get me wrong. I’m grateful for everything I have this holiday—my family, my loved ones, and the good food on our table, things that many people don’t even have. Still, I find myself pitying my life as I spend the rest of my vacation in my room—bed rotting, doom scrolling, and watching my shows—while I see my friends receiving nice things and going out to different places. I can’t help but feel jealous of the experiences they get to have, the ones I know I won't be able to have. I never felt this way when I was a kid. Back then, I was happy just playing outside with friends. Now, I keep longing for something more. I don’t think it’s wrong to desire those things, either. We all want something greater for ourselves. Even the poor March sisters longed for these (iykyk), and I just get them. All I could do is wish and hope that maybe, sooner or later, I'll get to have nice stuff and experience those things too. And if you ever felt this way too, just remember that everything will be alright. Maybe it's not now, but it will.

Since it’s the season of change (New Year) is coming, I pasted here a part of a diary entry from a day this December: It’s the nth of December and I still feel this looming sense of guilt and holiday melancholy that, I’m sure, not only me is having this season. I haven’t been doing much activities lately. My family isn’t really the “celebratory” type. I belong to the family category where we would just stay at home, do chores, and live like how we’ve been living for the rest of the year— indifferent of the way the rest of the world’s celebrating right now. It’s honestly kind of sad to not have grown up in a family opposite to ours. But what can I do? At least it’s not too late to change things. Now that we have kids at home (my nieces), we’re trying to change things up a bit to make them have a different experience. As simple as they may be, living in moments when you’re a kid feels a lot more magical and eventful than when you’re a grown up. I don’t want to make this too long, but I just want to address how much I’ve changed throughout the year— only noticing some parts of me that I’m now proud about— and learning more of myself, from the littlest to the grandest degree of self-awareness. I recently learn that to actually transform yourself to an entirely new person— more disciplined, more confident, kinder, etc.— sticking on a constant routine, buying self-improvement books, or waking up at 5 AM every morning wouldn’t be enough. These are all external factors that may crumble to pieces once we subconsciously get back to our old ways. What we need, according to that one article, is a change in identity— a metamorphosis of our inner subconscious and beliefs of who we are as a person. Don’t say “I will” and instead say “I am.” You ARE what you WILL become. Manifest it early on, make it your identity, and maybe, once and for all, you’ll finally be what you’re always meant to be. Happy New Year... and Happy New You! 🌷

‎Lately, I’ve been hearing a lot of people warning others not to become performative activists. I get the idea na activism should be deeply rooted, principled, and not reduced to aesthetics or clout. But honestly, the way this is often said feels less like a critique and more like a veiled attack. ‎ ‎What bothers me is this: regardless of whether someone is “performative” or not, they are still showing up, still speaking out publicly, still risking backlash and danger. That already means something... so why does it feel like activists are more eager to police each other than to confront the systems they claim to oppose? Bakit parang kayo-kayo lang din ang nagbababaan? ‎ ‎And genuine question, how exactly do you decide that someone is performative? Do you know their intentions? Their process? Their history? Or is it enough that they don’t meet your personal standard of what a “real” activist should look like? ‎ ‎I want to understand where this narrative is coming from, because from where I’m standing, it often feels like moral gatekeeping disguised as political consciousness. As if activism has turned into a purity test, where one wrong move, one imperfect expression, and you’re immediately dismissed. ‎ ‎I’m not an activist, I’m mostly quiet. But even from the outside, it’s hard not to notice how aggressively this label is thrown around, like these “performative activists” are everywhere, and like shaming them somehow strengthens the movement. If anything, it feels like it does the opposite. ‎ ‎Also, to be honest, I’m somewhat worried about how the label of “performative activism” is being used. Dati, madalas [REDACTED_NAME] [REDACTED_NAME] “performative” lang kayo sa pinaglalaban niyo from people who are openly anti-activism. [REDACTED_NAME] sa point na nakaka-discourage sa mga bagong aktibista o sa mga gustong mag-step out of their comfort zones at matuto, [REDACTED_NAME] participation at growth kaysa perfection [REDACTED_NAME]. ‎ ‎I hope this gets posted, not to provoke, but to invite conversation from different perspectives and to better understand how others see this issue.

This explainer outlines legally valid bases for rent setting and rent increases, particularly for private residential and student housing. 1. Rent Control Applies Only to Certain Units Under existing rent control laws: Rent regulation applies only to residential units below a specific monthly rent threshold Units priced above that threshold are legally exempt What this means legally If a rental unit is priced above the covered ceiling, rent is determined by agreement between landlord and tenant There is no statutory cap on increases for exempt units This is a lawful distinction explicitly provided for under national legislation. 2. Rent Increases Apply Only to Continuing Tenants Rent increase limits, where applicable, are tied to ongoing tenancy. Legally valid principle When a tenant vacates a unit, the landlord may: Set a new base rent for the next tenant Reflect current market conditions, costs, or improvements There is no legal prohibition against adjusting rent between different tenants. 3. Rental Contracts Are Governed by Freedom to Contract Philippine civil law recognizes the freedom of parties to enter into contracts. Legal implication Lease terms, including rent amount and escalation clauses, are valid if: Entered into voluntarily Not contrary to law, morals, or public policy Once agreed upon, contracts are presumed binding and enforceable A landlord is entitled to rely on written lease agreements duly accepted by tenants. 4. Utilities and Other Charges Are Not Considered “Rent” Rent control laws regulate monthly rent, not other expenses. Legally distinct items Electricity Water Internet Maintenance or service fees Furnishing or amenity charges As long as these charges are disclosed and agreed upon, they are lawful and separate from rent. 5. Property Classification Matters Not all housing arrangements fall under traditional residential leasing. Depending on structure and use, a property may be classified as: A boarding house A dormitory-style rental A lodging or bedspace arrangement Legal effect Some arrangements fall outside strict residential lease regulation Classification is based on actual use and contract terms, not assumptions 6. No Automatic Rent Approval or Registration Is Required There is currently: No requirement to register rent amounts with local governments No requirement to seek approval before adjusting rent Legal reality Rent regulation is complaint-based In the absence of a complaint or adverse ruling, rent agreements stand as valid 7. Rent Adjustments May Reflect Legitimate Cost Increases Landlords are legally entitled to consider: Rising utility costs Property maintenance and repairs Property taxes and compliance costs Inflation and market demand Provided increases comply with applicable laws, economic justification is lawful. 8. Enforcement Requires Due Process Any claim of illegal rent increase requires: A formal complaint Evidence of coverage under rent control Due process before any penalty is imposed Until such determination, rental terms remain presumed legal. Key Takeaways From a legal standpoint: -Rent control laws are limited in scope -Exemptions and thresholds are explicitly allowed -Contracts freely entered into are presumed valid -Landlords may lawfully adjust rent within these parameters -Understanding these boundaries ensures compliance while respecting property rights under Philippine law.

why do we beautify yearning? i get it. it's cinematic, but not loud; nowhere grand enough to be overwhelming; tender in its exterior. but that's the point... yearning is all superficial romance. when we peel off the silence, what is there to find? only a one-way avenue of pride and wanting. we love yearning because it's a manageable fantasy: one can revel in the flattery of being wanted, and the other can make company with someone merely imaginary. that's safe, sure—but still, isn't that abhorrently asymmetrical? yearning trades accountability for freedom. it thrives in the absence of clarity. there's nothing noble in expecting warmth from a vacuum. love should be connection, not projection. only mutual paranoia grows on a constant need-to-know basis. to love, truly so, means to risk rather than keeping some poor soul at arm's length (or being content in the inverse)! yearning is far too safe and juvenile to be called love. 🫶 - b24 byes-xual

Why is the default response always to make exams harder instead of fixing the program? When students struggle, the solution shouldn’t be “double your effort and hope you survive.” That’s not rigor — that’s poor system design. Harder exams don’t automatically mean better education, better vets, or better board results. They just mean more burnout. If pass rates drop, that’s feedback. It’s not a challenge to raise the difficulty even more. At some point, we have to ask: are we actually teaching better, or just filtering harder? Education should train competence, not glorify suffering. If the method isn’t working anymore, maybe it’s time to change the method — not blame the students.

You tell yourself to be mature, to let things pass. But slowly, the hurt stops coming from one big moment and starts coming from many small ones—each making you feel a little less chosen, a little more replaceable. It feels odd, weird even. I should be celebrating my acheivements for actually being able to strive and outdo myself in all expectations, especially my own. I was so happy over my academic performance as a freshie who really did so well for "their first semester", everyone I talked to about me being able to achieve the requirements of college scholar, even while I was doubting my capabilities to keep up with the speed of the hectic semester, congratulated me with warm arms, and the motivation to do better only got stronger with the comfort and recognition of friends and family. I was happy, proud of myself, and I realized that I really can do it if I just believed in myself! But why is it so bittersweet? But am I thinking that there was just this "isang suntok nalang" instead of "i actually did it!" Why do i feel disappointed but at the same time so happy and relieved? Is it because I was this close to being a university scholar in my first semester as a freshie? Or was it because I knew deep down I couldve done so much better in this semester if I just didnt make the clumsy mistakes I did. Regardless, the experience is both a fever dream and a humbling one. I am glad that im taking this somewhat lightheartedly irl, not crying or extremely heartbroken about this, because for me, I know it takes "one bawi" and maybe just better management to achieve such greatness, but it hurts a little, to know that I could've done it then and there. It sucks, but hey, I got what I wanted, I dont want to get over myself. atleast the first of my many fights is finally completed, and I can finally rest well in the comfort of my home again. - iloveblackholes

UTAK at PUSO For years, lagi kong iniisip Utak o Puso. I remember being asked ano pipiliin [REDACTED_NAME]. I said Utak. But I never really excelled at academics. I failed a lot of quizzes and exams. I never topped any of my subjects. I knew, deep down, I had to appear brilliant to earn validation from professors and peers. I graduated without a medal. Nakakahiya kasi halos lahat [REDACTED_NAME] grumaduate ay marami ang medals. I took the board exam, but didn't even get a high score. I dreamed of being in the Top 10, but I never did. [REDACTED_NAME] 'yon, [REDACTED_NAME]. I chose Utak because it was easier to measure. It was easier to find validation from grades, rankings, and honors, especially in UP where brilliance is often rewarded and takes center stage. But I realized I slowly neglected something else. I neglected Puso. Because Puso is not flashy on tests. It doesn't appear on transcripts. It always worked behind the scenes. I asked, "What good does it do in an environment where shining on stage matters so much?" Now, I choose Puso. Not because Utak is unimportant. I let Puso rise to the surface because Utak alone wasn't enough to define me. All those times I felt embarrassed for not being the brightest student, I never realized I should've excelled in things that hold our communities together. The way we listen The way we become someone's safe space The way we understand The way we FEEL. Medals are impressive. High grades and Latin Honors deserve applause. But we rarely give recognition to people who gave us light when things were dark. People will forget your grades on a test, but they will never forget how you made them feel.

Regarding Did you write that article? If so, be ashamed. The thing is they achieved something great which should first and foremost be the highlight of the article and that was blatantly ignored in favor of pity points, excuses and reasons. You have not only disrespected the achievements of the students athletes by failing to put the spotlight on their wins but you have also insulted their competitive spirit and the essence of competition. Speaking as a former student athlete myself, all you lot wrote about was one big excuse, a farm for pity points that can be taken as an excuse for poor performances. I look at this article through the perspective of a competitor and think "Oh so our student athletes probably didn't do so well. [REDACTED_NAME] behind the scenes and sa personal lives so I won't expect good results." But that was not the case. They did well, they did amazing IN SPITE of the struggles. You have defined them by the generalized difficulties which you have lazily branded on all of them. Do all of them struggle with academics? Are they all low on funds? You couldn't cover everything but you know of their struggles and difficulties or are we generalizing? Might as well change their title to "struggling-student athletes" instead. You have taken away their spotlight in favor of this so called intentional storytelling. Imagine how much more inspiring would it have been if the article highlighted their achievements and then mentioned the behind the scenes struggles, tell people what they managed to achieve despite the difficulties and then finish with a call to action. "Imagine what our athletes could do with better support and resources." Not just bad journalism, it is lazy journalism. Tell their stories, ALL their stories.

This is a direct response to . Calling the STRASUC article “unclear” or “half-baked” kind of misses what it was trying to do. Opening with student-athlete struggles wasn’t some lazy or overused move—it was a deliberate choice, and a relevant one. Those struggles are real, they’re ongoing, and they’re a huge part of understanding what these athletes actually accomplished. Dismissing that angle as “overused” ignores how hard it really is to balance academics, training, injuries, travel, and mental pressure all at once. Talking about those struggles gives more meaning to their wins. It puts a human face to the results instead of just listing numbers. And honestly, that context matters just as much as stats do. Sports writing isn’t only about medal counts—it’s also about growth, experience, and momentum. The complaint that the article didn’t mention every gold medal or record assumes one piece has to cover everything, which just isn’t realistic. Focusing on a few athletes or moments doesn’t erase the achievements of others, and it definitely doesn’t take away from big wins like the men’s badminton team’s gold or the swimmer who won eight gold medals. Those accomplishments are important, sure—but not mentioning them in one article doesn’t automatically make the articel disrespectful. Calling the piece disrespectful also ignores the reality of timing and constraints. The piece chose to spotlight the human side of competition, and that choice is just as valid as a numbers-heavy recap. If anything, the STRASUC article reminded readers that these athletes are more than medal counts. They’re students who went through real struggles and still managed to do better even with their academics in line. That’s not bad journalism—that’s intentional storytelling. Now, what is wrong with that?

I have failed an exam or two on my previous courses pero instead of h*ting the course, I decided to do better. 3. Study to understand. Always try to understand rather than memorize, even if concepts [REDACTED_NAME] or theories ang tinuturo try to connect them to real life situations. 4. Always give yourself time to rest. Hindi ka [REDACTED_NAME] okay. I’ve done shit on an exam before because I was too drained to study for it (pinili [REDACTED_NAME])... My flat uno standing turned into a 1.75 because of it, pero instead of paghihinayang, I just felt relieved which gets me to my next point 5. DON’T BE TOO HARD ON YOURSELF. Lahat tayo matalino. We may not all just be on the same level, pero lahat tayo may maibubuga. Don’t compare yourself to others. Kung naka flat uno siya pero 1.5 ka lang, still celebrate! [REDACTED_NAME]. These are just a few tips and tricks I’ve picked up on ever since I entered UP ;> Good luck to us next sem!

As a former sports and feature writer, what was that STRASUC article about? The flow and angle were so unclear; it felt like multiple points were just pasted together. They could have compared the medal tally and performances of each team to last year, but they didn't. They chose the most cliché struggle of a student-athlete for an intro, then suddenly shifted to the history of some of the athletes. Also, I'm pretty sure UP finished 6th overall in the Olympics, not 5th. I just feel bad for the student-athletes at this point. As far as I know, all of the men's badminton team won gold. Isn't that worth mentioning? One of the swimmers also set a record by winning 8 gold medals in all 8 of his events. Isn't that worth mentioning? They went through so much and achieved so much for the university, yet the only coverage they get is some photos and a half-baked article. I understand that the semester and the year are already over, and everyone just wants to relax and finish everything up. But that felt so disrespectful to all the other student-athletes who were barely represented or not represented at all. Do better, UP.
