One of my classmate said to another classmate that “If you believe in same-s*x marriage, why are you calling yourself a [REDACTED_NAME]?”. The question assumes that Christianity has only one correct definition that everyone agrees on. From an atheist perspective, that assumption doesn’t hold up and ends up excluding people rather than encouraging meaningful discussion. To begin with, Christianity isn’t a single, uniform belief system. There are thousands of denominations, and many of them openly support same-s*x marriage. Since Christians themselves disagree on how to interpret the Bible, it seems unreasonable for any one person or group to decide who is or isn’t a “real” [REDACTED_NAME] based on a single belief. Supporting same-s*x marriage alone doesn’t erase someone’s [REDACTED_NAME] identity. This argument also depends on picking and choosing which parts of scripture must be taken literally. Religious texts have always been shaped by history, culture, and translation. Over time, Christians have reinterpreted teachings about slavery, the role of women, and interracial marriage. From an atheist point of view, it’s inconsistent to insist that views on homos*xuality are the only ones that can never be reexamined. Support for same-s*x marriage is also grounded in basic ideas like fairness, consent, and equal treatment—values that don’t depend on religion. A [REDACTED_NAME] who supports same-s*x marriage may simply be applying those values alongside core [REDACTED_NAME] teachings about love, compassion, and justice. Rather than weakening faith, this shows how moral understanding can grow and adapt. Ultimately, this argument turns religion into a tool for gatekeeping instead of guidance. If Christianity claims to be centered on love and inclusion, then denying someone’s faith identity because they support equal rights contradicts those principles. From an atheist perspective, the real issue isn’t a conflict in belief, but how religion is being used to exclude rather than understand. (from another campus)

Clicking Pen With just a click, you stir my mind, A spark that lingers, undefined. I never thought a sound so small Could pull my heart, could bind it all. I ask my friends what this could be, They say, “It’s nothing, just a tease.” Yet in that sound, a mystery, A silent code I long to seize. I ask another, “Is it mere habit?” They smile and say, “No meaning there.” But every click feels like a magnet, A hidden thread between us rare. When eyes collide, the world is still, A fleeting hush, a secret thrill. A quiet whisper, soft, profound, A deeper voice without a sound. I catch your gaze, it feels intense, Yet I stay still, lost in suspense. You’ll never find me interesting, We’re too far apart, I’m not your being. And in the silence, I quietly dream, Each tiny click, a coded theme. A gentle pull, a secret stream, Guiding me closer than it may seem. 🍳🌊

As this semester comes to a close, I want to express my deepest gratitude to Doc, whose golden heart and genuine compassion for students have made a remarkable impact on us. Despite the final standings, you still chose to open doors for students to pass the subject through alternative ways—whether through a removal exam, an oral exam, or any other form of evaluation that allows us to demonstrate what we truly know. This kind of understanding is something I deeply appreciate. Not every learning journey is linear. Not every student fits perfectly into the confines of written exams or rigid grading systems. But professors like you remind us that education is not just about numbers; it is about people. It is about recognizing potential, giving chances, and believing that students can grow when given the right support. Doc, thank you. Thank you for being the kind of educator who ensures no one is left behind, who always finds ways to help us survive—and even thrive—in your class. Your kindness, flexibility, and dedication show what true teaching really looks like. We are grateful for your heart, your patience, and your willingness to see our struggles beyond the surface. To our dear educators, I hope more professors embrace this kind of approach. Students are not defined solely by exam results or final percentages. Behind every grade is a story—of effort, challenges, personal battles, and growth. When a professor opens alternative pathways for students to pass—be it a removal exam, an oral assessment, or another constructive method—it does not diminish academic integrity. Instead, it strengthens it by acknowledging diverse forms of learning and by giving students the opportunity to prove their understanding in ways that may suit them better. Every student has potential. Sometimes, all they need is a professor willing to see it. Not through grades alone, but through effort, improvement, and the willingness to rise when given the chance. I hope more educators adopt this mindset—one that is compassionate, fair, and inclusive. Because at the end of the day, the goal of education is not to filter students out, but to guide them, uplift them, and help them become the best versions of themselves. To Doc and to all professors who go the extra mile for their students—thank you. Your impact goes beyond the classroom. Your kindness is remembered long after the grades are forgotten. 🌟 -Iskx -XXX
